host-2017-verb1-ELF-031.solution_path: The 'Women-only Cabs' passage flags two 'speed bumps' for SheTaxis. One is practical: 'Demand has been so great that the firm has had to decelerate its launch until it can recruit 500 drivers.' Passenger interest is outrunning supply — that matches D, unexpected success.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt is 'What are we told in relation to SheTaxis?' — a factual-claim question. You need the option that matches something the passage explicitly says about SheTaxis, not something it merely hints at. Scan for concrete claims, not vibes.

steps.title: Locate the key sentence
steps.text: The passage names two 'speed bumps'. The practical one is the load-bearing sentence: 'Demand has been so great that the firm has had to decelerate its launch until it can recruit 500 drivers.' Slowing down a launch because demand is too high is a very particular kind of problem — a good problem.

steps.title: Paraphrase in plain English
steps.text: More riders want SheTaxis than SheTaxis can serve. The firm has had to deliberately slow its rollout while it recruits enough women drivers to meet that demand. The bottleneck is supply, not interest.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Speed bumps' = obstacles, slowdowns. 'Decelerate' = slow down — the same root as 'accelerate'. 'Vice versa' = the other way round (here: male passengers can also request male drivers). These are everyday business-section idioms; they don't carry hidden meaning.

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: Walk each option against the paraphrase. A says women deserve a chance to drive yellow cabs — the passage reports statistics, not an opinion call. B says male colleagues are sceptical — the passage never describes how male drivers feel. C says male passengers won't accept female drivers — but SheTaxis excludes male passengers entirely (only female passengers can use it), so their reaction is off-topic. D ('unexpected success among passengers') maps cleanly onto 'demand has been so great that the firm has had to decelerate its launch'.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is D. The passage tells us one concrete thing about SheTaxis as a business: passenger demand has been so strong that the company is rationing its own growth. That is the very definition of unexpected commercial success.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read the '99% male' statistic as a complaint that women have been kept out of the cab business too long, and to hear A as the moral the passage is drawing.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage reports a fact, not a verdict. It never frames the gender split as overdue or unjust — those are values A imports from outside. Step 3's paraphrase contains only supply and demand, no ethical claim.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at the line 'SheTaxis is obviously discriminating against men' and infer that male drivers must resent the firm or treat its drivers with hostility.
distractors.why_wrong: The legal problem is statutory anti-discrimination law, not workplace culture among drivers. The passage says nothing about how male colleagues react to SheTaxis drivers — step 5 catches the leap from law to colleague behaviour.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on a gender-segregated service is to ask the symmetric question: how will men feel? It sounds like a natural angle.
distractors.why_wrong: SheTaxis doesn't carry male passengers — they aren't customers of the service. Their acceptance is structurally irrelevant to SheTaxis's success, so the text never raises it.

host-2017-verb1-ELF-031.technique: On 'what are we told' factual-claim items, find the sentence that names a CONCRETE PROBLEM or RESULT and match the option to that fact. The trigger is the phrase 'has been so great' / 'has had to' — a signal of unexpected scale.
host-2017-verb1-ELF-031.pitfall: Distractors here import outside angles (moral verdicts, male colleague reactions, male passenger acceptance) that sound plausible because they fit the topic. The cure: every option must be traceable to a specific sentence in the passage — if you cannot quote a line, the option is overreach.

host-2017-verb1-ELF-032.solution_path: The writer notes that 'anti-discrimination law is not always applied with common sense, that may be illegal' — implying SheTaxis's policy is sensible in spirit even if the letter of the law might still trip it up. That matches B: the writer thinks the firm conforms to the spirit of the law.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt asks about the writer's ATTITUDE — what tone or stance the writer takes toward SheTaxis. Attitude questions live in the writer's word choices, not in the surface facts. Watch for loaded adjectives, hedges, and ironic phrasing.

steps.title: Locate the writer's voice
steps.text: The decisive line is the last one: 'Since anti-discrimination law is not always applied with common sense, that may be illegal.' Two things sit inside it: a sympathetic remark about common sense, and a dry 'may be' acknowledging the legal risk. The writer is siding with common sense, not with the law as written.

steps.title: Paraphrase the writer's stance
steps.text: What the writer is really saying: 'A reasonable reading of anti-discrimination law would let SheTaxis through, but the law is sometimes applied unreasonably, so they might still get caught.' The complaint is aimed at over-literal law, not at SheTaxis.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Common sense' = sensible, reasonable judgement — a positive frame. 'May be illegal' is a hedge: not 'is illegal' but 'might be ruled illegal'. 'Spirit of the law' (used in option B) means what the law intends, as opposed to its letter — exactly what 'applied with common sense' invokes.

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: Walk each option. A says the writer worries about prejudice against existing female drivers — the writer never frames the 4% statistic as prejudice; it's just a setup. B ('conforms to the spirit of the law') matches the writer's 'common sense' framing of the policy. C says the writer is upset about discrimination against male cab drivers — but the writer's tone toward that point is detached and slightly mocking, not 'upset'. D says the writer expects the law to be rewritten in favour of SheTaxis — the writer hopes for sensible application, not for a new statute.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is B. The writer's complaint is that the LAW isn't always applied with common sense — implying that SheTaxis would pass a common-sense (spirit-of-the-law) test, even if a literal reading might still trip it up.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read the opening statistic (99% male, 4% even by booking app) as the writer warning about a hostile climate toward women drivers and to assume A captures the concern.
distractors.why_wrong: Those statistics are scene-setting, not a complaint. Step 2 shows the writer's actual voice surfaces only at the end, around the law and common sense — not around prejudice.

distractors.why_tempting: If you read 'SheTaxis is obviously discriminating against men' as the writer's own indignation, C feels like the right tone — sympathy with the excluded majority.
distractors.why_wrong: 'Obviously' here is sardonic — the writer is conceding the literal legal claim while signalling it's the kind of claim that misses the point. The follow-up about 'common sense' makes the irony plain.

distractors.why_tempting: Left-to-right reading turns 'that may be illegal' into a prediction the law will need fixing, and D reads that as 'the law will be changed in favour of SheTaxis'.
distractors.why_wrong: The writer hopes the existing law will be applied sensibly, not that a new law will replace it. D imports a legislative change that the passage never proposes — step 3's paraphrase keeps the writer's actual stance in view.

host-2017-verb1-ELF-032.technique: On 'writer's attitude' items, find a hedge ('may be', 'not always', 'apparently') or an evaluative adverb ('obviously', 'unfortunately') — that word usually carries the stance. The trigger pattern: a hedge in the final sentence often delivers the verdict the question is testing.
host-2017-verb1-ELF-032.pitfall: Attitude questions tempt you to read the writer's tone as identical to the surface facts of the article. The cure: separate what the writer REPORTS (existing statistics, the firm's launch) from how the writer FRAMES it (common sense, may-be illegal). Only the framing answers an attitude question.

host-2017-verb1-ELF-033.solution_path: The first two paragraphs say that in the U.S. 'the percentage of the U.S. population that proclaims its religious affiliation as "none" has more than doubled, to about 15 percent', and that many of these people 'conduct their life outside the religious establishment'. That implies fewer Americans see themselves as actively involved in religion — answer D.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt asks what is IMPLIED in the first two paragraphs about religious belief. 'Implied' is wider than 'stated' — you're looking for a claim that follows from the passage's wording, not necessarily one quoted directly. Scope is also restricted: only the first two paragraphs count.

steps.title: Locate the key sentences
steps.text: Two sentences carry the implication. 'In the past two decades, the percentage of the U.S. population that proclaims its religious affiliation as "none" has more than doubled, to about 15 percent.' And: 'Many of these individuals do not identify as atheists; even so, many of them conduct their life outside the religious establishment.'

steps.title: Paraphrase in plain English
steps.text: Twenty years ago, fewer than 7% of Americans claimed no religious affiliation; now about 15% do. Most of those people aren't full-blown atheists — they just don't take part in organised religion. So actively involved religion is shrinking in the U.S., even if outright disbelief is not the same as that shrinkage.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Religious affiliation' = the religious group a person identifies with. 'Religious establishment' = organised religion, churches and institutions. 'Atheist' = someone who positively denies the existence of God — a stronger claim than just 'none'. 'Imply' = suggest without saying outright; what follows logically from what is said.

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: Walk each option. A says atheists are about to outnumber religious people in America — too strong; 15% is a doubling, not a majority, and most of those aren't atheists anyway. B says research shows religiosity is the SINGLE most important factor for well-being — the paragraphs say 'as compared with religious individuals, people who lack a creed are less likely to be happy', not that religion is THE single factor. C says religious faith is losing ground in MOST parts of the world — paragraphs 1-2 only discuss the U.S.; the global picture comes later. D ('Fewer people than before in the U.S. now consider themselves actively involved in religion') matches the doubled 'none' percentage and the 'outside the religious establishment' line.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is D. The passage carefully distinguishes 'no affiliation' from 'atheism' — and the shift it implies is a decline in active religious participation, not in belief itself. That is exactly what D names.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to compress 'non-believers number between 500 million and 750 million' plus a doubling of the 'none' category into a story about atheists taking over America.
distractors.why_wrong: The 500–750 million figure is global, not American, and the passage explicitly says many of the 'none' group 'do not identify as atheists'. Step 3 keeps the distinction visible: 'none' is not the same as 'atheist'.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on the opening line — 'people who lack a creed are less likely to be happy' — is to read religion as the single biggest factor in well-being.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage names religion as one factor, not THE factor. Later paragraphs (outside scope here) actually argue that societal conditions matter more — but even within the first two paragraphs, nothing claims a single overriding factor.

distractors.why_tempting: If you remember the line about 'shifting beliefs of a growing number of people' as a worldwide trend, C reads as the natural generalisation.
distractors.why_wrong: Paragraphs 1 and 2 only document the U.S. — the global poll arrives later. Step 1's scope reminder (only the first two paragraphs) catches this. C overreaches geographically.

host-2017-verb1-ELF-033.technique: On 'what is implied' items, separate the passage's explicit statistics from the conclusions that follow from them. Look for a contrast or a clarifying 'even so' ('many do not identify as atheists; EVEN SO, many conduct their life outside the religious establishment') — the implication usually lives in that clarifier.
host-2017-verb1-ELF-033.pitfall: Three of the wrong options here ride a single word too far: A inflates 'none' into 'atheist', B inflates 'a factor' into 'the single most important factor', C inflates 'U.S.' into 'most parts of the world'. The cure: when an option contains a quantifier (single most, most, all, no one), find the matching word in the passage and check whether it's actually that strong.

host-2017-verb1-ELF-034.solution_path: The passage says 'whether a person was struggling or thriving was nowhere near as predictive of religiosity as a society's conditions and norms' and quotes Ed Diener: 'societal forces led to religiosity rather than individual forces.' Recent research keeps finding that the religion–well-being link only appears WHEN society makes it appear — a social factor. That matches B.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt asks what is SAID about recent research on the relationship between religion and well-being. 'Said' is more direct than 'implied' — you want a claim the passage states fairly explicitly about the research findings, not a downstream inference.

steps.title: Locate the key sentences
steps.text: Two passages do the heavy lifting. State level: 'Religious people may be happier than their godless counterparts, but only if the society they belong to values religion highly.' Global level: 'Whether a person was struggling or thriving was nowhere near as predictive of religiosity as a society's conditions and norms.' Both pivot on 'society'.

steps.title: Paraphrase in plain English
steps.text: Religion only seems to make people happier in places where religion is highly valued. In secular places, religious people aren't notably happier than non-religious ones. So the link between faith and happiness depends on the SOCIAL context, not on faith alone.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Correlation' = a statistical relationship between two things (here: religiosity and well-being). 'Subjective well-being' = how a person rates their own happiness or life satisfaction. 'Societal forces' = pressures that come from the surrounding society rather than from a single person's choice or circumstances.

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: Walk each option. A says recent research has CONFIRMED intuitive views — the opposite of the finding; the new research is contrasted with 'previous studies' that simply said religion helps. B ('a main finding has been the importance of social factors for a correlation to appear') maps directly onto 'society's conditions and norms' and Diener's quote about societal forces. C says methodological problems made results controversial — the passage describes the methodology favourably ('representative sample', '154 countries') with no mention of controversy. D says secular societies provide LESS social contentment than religious ones — the actual finding is that in easy living conditions, both groups have 'similar, relatively high subjective well-being'.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is B. The headline of the recent research is that society's stance toward religion — not individual belief — is what makes the religion-and-happiness correlation appear at all.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read the early line 'people who lack a creed are less likely to be happy and healthy' as the conclusion the new research confirms, since it's the most quotable claim in the opening.
distractors.why_wrong: That early line summarises PREVIOUS research; the new research complicates it by adding the societal condition. Step 3's paraphrase makes the contrast visible: old story = religion helps; new story = religion helps WHEN society values religion.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'examining it with greater resolution' and assume the older research must have had methodological problems that the new work is fixing.
distractors.why_wrong: 'Greater resolution' here means a finer-grained sample, not a fix for past errors. The passage never says results are controversial — only that they now break down by location, which is added detail, not contested findings.

distractors.why_tempting: If you remember 'people in less religious parts of the country' as 'people in less happy parts of the country', D's claim that secular societies are less contented sounds correct.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage says the opposite for wealthy secular areas: people there have higher subjective well-being than people in poorer religious areas. Step 5 lines up the actual finding against D's inversion.

host-2017-verb1-ELF-034.technique: On 'what is said about the research' items, hunt for a contrast between 'previous studies' and 'recent studies' — the test usually lives in what changed. The trigger: words like 'For the first time…' or 'researchers have collected' typically announce the new finding.
host-2017-verb1-ELF-034.pitfall: It's easy to merge the older intuitive view (religion → happier) with the new nuanced finding (religion → happier IF society values religion). The cure: write down both views separately before matching options, so distractors that quote only the old view stand out as outdated.

host-2017-verb1-ELF-035.solution_path: Diener's U.S. data shows that in religious states like Mississippi, religion does make people happier, BUT those people 'were worse off than the contented residents of more affluent states, where religion mattered less.' So secular wealthy states beat religious poor ones on well-being — answer A.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt asks about three variables at once — religiosity, well-being, relative wealth — in the United States according to Diener. So you need an option that gets all three relationships right, not just two. Watch each option as a triple.

steps.title: Locate the key sentences
steps.text: Two consecutive sentences answer the question. 'In the states where religion was very important, people were much more likely to be living in difficult circumstances. They also had lower subjective well-being than people living in less religious parts of the country.' And: 'Did religion make them happier… ? Absolutely, according to the data – but they were worse off than the contented residents of more affluent states, where religion mattered less.'

steps.title: Paraphrase in plain English
steps.text: Religious U.S. states tend to be poor states. Inside those poor states, religious people do feel better than their non-religious neighbours. But step back, and the affluent secular states beat the poor religious states overall. So the order is: rich-secular happiest, then poor-religious, then poor-non-religious.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Affluent' = wealthy. 'Subjective well-being' = self-rated happiness. 'Religiosity' = the degree to which someone or a population is religious. 'Regardless of material status' (which appears in B and D) means 'no matter how rich or poor' — be alert: the passage's whole point is that material status MATTERS, so options that ignore it usually lose.

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: Walk each option. A ('People in relatively wealthy states are less religious but more contented than religious people in poorer states') matches the second key sentence word for word: contented residents of more affluent states where religion mattered less, vs. people in religious-but-difficult states. B says religious people are more contented REGARDLESS of material status — passage explicitly says material status matters. C inverts A: poor states are MORE contented than rich ones — the opposite of the data. D says non-religious people are more contented regardless of material status — also ignores material status, and inside any one state, religion does still help.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is A. Within a poor religious state, religion helps; but across the whole country, the secular wealthy states top the well-being ranking. A captures that cross-state comparison exactly.

distractors.why_tempting: If you remember only the early framing — 'religious people may be happier' — and miss the cross-state comparison, B sounds like the right summary: religion wins everywhere.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage explicitly conditions that claim on material status: 'they were worse off than the contented residents of more affluent states'. Step 3's paraphrase ranks the four groups; B flattens that ranking by ignoring wealth.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on the phrase 'religion made them happier' can be to extend it across all comparisons, including poor-vs-rich. C reads as the religion-wins story applied to states.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage rules this out directly: in religious (poorer) states 'they had lower subjective well-being than people living in less religious parts of the country'. C inverts the comparison that the passage actually reports.

distractors.why_tempting: Snap-reading the contrast as 'wealthy secular states beat religious poor ones' can collapse into 'non-religious always wins'.
distractors.why_wrong: Within a single state, religion still helps (the data on Mississippi confirms it). And 'regardless of material status' contradicts the passage's central point. Step 5's triple — religiosity, well-being, wealth — keeps D from sliding through.

host-2017-verb1-ELF-035.technique: On items that ask about THREE variables at once, write the three out in a row before matching options, and check each option for all three. The trigger: when an option contains 'regardless of…' or 'no matter…', that's a flag — the passage usually has a condition, and options that drop the condition usually lose.
host-2017-verb1-ELF-035.pitfall: There are two true claims in the paragraph and they pull in opposite directions: religion helps WITHIN a poor state, but the wealthy secular states still win OVERALL. The cure: hold both claims in view and pick the option that uses BOTH (poorer-and-religious vs. wealthier-and-secular).

host-2017-verb1-ELF-036.solution_path: Diener's global study says: 'If you live in a nation where daily existence is difficult, your life satisfaction is generally lower. In those countries, being more religious appears to grant you a premium on happiness that your less religious neighbours do not enjoy.' Poor countries reward religiosity with extra happiness — exactly B.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt asks which conclusion about Ed Diener's GLOBAL study is true — so the answer must come from the 154-country survey, not the U.S. state data discussed earlier. Watch out for options that quote U.S.-only findings.

steps.title: Locate the key sentences
steps.text: Three lines define the global picture. 'A tough environment also tended to coincide with greater national religiosity.' 'In those countries, being more religious appears to grant you a premium on happiness that your less religious neighbours do not enjoy.' And the contrast: 'If the living is easy, however, both nonbelievers and religious people have similar, relatively high subjective well-being.'

steps.title: Paraphrase in plain English
steps.text: In countries where life is hard, religious people are noticeably happier than non-religious people in the same country. In countries where life is easy, the two groups are about equally happy. So religion buys an extra dose of happiness — but only in hard-luck nations.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Premium' here means a bonus, an extra amount — as in 'paying a premium' for something. 'Tough environment' = harsh living conditions: poverty, instability, lack of services. 'Boosted' (in option D) means lifted up, increased — a stronger claim than the passage's hedged 'appears to grant'.

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: Walk each option. A says religion makes little difference 'whatever the circumstances' — too sweeping; in hard-living countries it does make a difference. B ('in poor countries, religious individuals enjoy a greater sense of well-being than non-religious ones') matches 'a premium on happiness that your less religious neighbours do not enjoy' in tough environments. C says all religions occasionally fail to promote national well-being — the passage actually says the effect held true for ALL religions in the sample, so this inverts the finding. D says religion boosts happiness REGARDLESS of country — directly contradicted by the 'similar' well-being in easy-living countries.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is B. The global study's headline finding is conditional: in poor countries with tough lives, religion really does deliver a happiness premium over non-religion. In easy-living countries that premium disappears.

distractors.why_tempting: If you remember only the easy-living finding — 'nonbelievers and religious people have similar… well-being' — and miss the tough-environment half, A reads as the bottom line.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage explicitly says the picture flips in tough environments: religion DOES deliver a premium there. Step 3's paraphrase holds both halves of the contrast; A only uses one.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on the line 'this effect held true for all religions represented in the sample' can be to read 'effect' as 'failure to help', so C sounds like a careful summary.
distractors.why_wrong: 'Effect held true' means the same pattern (religion helps in tough places, neutral in easy ones) showed up across Buddhism, Christianity, Hinduism, Islam. It does NOT say any religion 'occasionally fails to promote national well-being'. Step 5 catches the inversion.

distractors.why_tempting: If you remember the line 'religious people may be happier than their godless counterparts', D's blanket claim of a religious boost can feel like the natural summary.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage qualifies that claim with 'only if the society they belong to values religion highly'. D drops the condition — and the easy-living countries show the boost disappearing when the condition isn't met.

host-2017-verb1-ELF-036.technique: On global-study items, look for the IF clause that limits a finding ('IF you live in a nation where daily existence is difficult…'). Options that drop the IF tend to be wrong; options that preserve it tend to be right. The trigger is a sentence that contrasts 'tough' / 'difficult' with 'easy' / 'affluent'.
host-2017-verb1-ELF-036.pitfall: When a study lists conditions on both sides ('in tough environments… in easy environments…'), it's tempting to pick the option that mentions only the half you noticed first. The cure: write both halves down before scoring options — that forces you to spot the half-truths.

host-2017-verb1-ELF-037.solution_path: The last two paragraphs note that societal forces — not personal belief — drive whether religion produces happiness, and conclude with 'atheists are not permanently shut off from some fountain of happiness'. The takeaway: religion alone does not guarantee happiness; the social context does most of the work. That matches D.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt asks what can be CONCLUDED from the last two paragraphs. 'Concluded' means a justified inference, not a direct quote — you need the option that follows from the closing argument as a whole, not from a single line.

steps.title: Locate the closing argument
steps.text: Two ideas anchor the close. First, Diener's quote: 'societal forces led to religiosity rather than individual forces.' Second, the conclusion to nonbelievers: 'believers will accrue more psychological benefits only in places that value religion more, and vice versa.' Religion only delivers happiness conditionally — it depends on the society around you.

steps.title: Paraphrase in plain English
steps.text: Being religious does not, by itself, make you happier. Whether religion translates into happiness depends on whether the surrounding society values religion. So if you were to ask 'does religion guarantee happiness?' the answer the passage points to is: no, not in itself.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Conclude' = derive a verdict from evidence. 'Bolster' = strengthen or support. 'Like-minded community' = a group of people who share your outlook — here, secular communities for non-believers. 'In itself' (option D) means 'by itself, without other conditions'.

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: Walk each option. A says well-being is UNRELATED to religiosity — too strong; in pro-religion societies religion does relate to well-being. B says atheism is a special form of religion — the passage talks about 'secular communities of like-minded people' offering similar social support, but never equates atheism with religion. C says high social status enhances religiosity — the passage actually links religiosity to TOUGH environments, the opposite direction. D ('religion in itself is no guarantee of happiness') matches both the Diener quote ('societal forces') and the 'only in places that value religion more' qualifier.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is D. The last two paragraphs build a single thesis: religion's link to happiness is conditional on society. Strip the social context away and the guarantee disappears — which is exactly what D names.

distractors.why_tempting: If you read 'societal forces led to religiosity rather than individual forces' as 'religion has no effect at all', A feels like the clean takeaway.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage shows religion DOES correlate with happiness in religious societies — what is variable is whether the religion–happiness link appears, not whether well-being can ever depend on religiosity. Step 3 keeps the conditional alive.

distractors.why_tempting: It's tempting to read 'secular communities of like-minded people can offer similar social support' as 'atheism functions like a religion'.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage notes that secular GROUPS can replicate the SOCIAL benefits — the community part — without claiming atheism is itself a form of religion. Step 5 catches this overreach: 'similar social support' ≠ 'a form of religion'.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on 'societal forces' can be to read 'higher social status' as a likely driver, since status is one obvious social variable.
distractors.why_wrong: The actual driver in the passage is the OPPOSITE: tough environments correlate with higher religiosity. C names a relationship that the passage never claims, and step 2's locator shows the direction goes from hardship → religiosity, not status → religiosity.

host-2017-verb1-ELF-037.technique: On 'what can be concluded' items with a multi-paragraph scope, look for the qualifier the author has been adding throughout ('only if', 'only in places that…') — the conclusion almost always preserves that qualifier. The trigger: a closing line that adds 'in itself' or 'depending on…' signals a conditional thesis.
host-2017-verb1-ELF-037.pitfall: It's easy to over-correct from 'religion definitely helps' to 'religion definitely doesn't help'. The cure: the passage's actual position is 'it depends on society' — pick the option that captures the IF, not options at either extreme.

host-2017-verb1-ELF-038.solution_path: The 'Cooking' paragraph praises Julie Powell for setting herself up for 'tightly scheduled failures on a regimented basis' so that 'any passing pleasure… will be overwhelmed by tedium and obligation. As it should be.' Praising tedium-as-virtue is dry mockery of cookery's hobby cult — the tone is sarcastic. Answer C.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt asks how the TONE in this text is best described. Tone questions live in word choice — adjectives, mock-praise, registers that don't match the topic. Read the passage as if listening to the writer's voice, not just collecting facts.

steps.title: Locate the tone signals
steps.text: The giveaway lines: 'Cooking is, in fact, characterised by duty, boredom, bossiness and chaos; it is life in microcosm.' And: 'set yourself up for tightly scheduled failures on a regimented basis. This ensures that any passing pleasure… will be overwhelmed by tedium and obligation. As it should be.' A writer who openly recommends tedium and failure is not being literal.

steps.title: Identify the rhetorical move
steps.text: The writer takes the opposite of what most cookery writing celebrates (joy, creativity, sensuality) and APPROVES of it: duty, boredom, regimented failure. Saying 'as it should be' after a list of negatives is mock-endorsement — the writer means the opposite of the surface claim. That move is called sarcasm or irony.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Sarcastic' = saying the opposite of what is meant, often with bitter or mocking edge. 'Sentimental' = full of warm feeling, often sweet or nostalgic. 'Prosaic' = plain, factual, unimaginative — flat. 'Moving' = emotionally stirring, touching. The writer's voice is sharp and ironic, not sweet, flat, or touching.

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: Walk each option. A 'Sentimental' — no warmth here; the writer is dismissive, not nostalgic. B 'Prosaic' — far too flat; phrases like 'knock cookery off the pedestal' are arch and showy, not unadorned. C 'Sarcastic' — matches the mock-endorsement of tedium and the closing 'as it should be'. D 'Moving' — the writer is dry and ironic, not seeking to stir emotion.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is C. The Cooking paragraph isn't a literal essay on chores — it's a sardonic poke at people who romanticise the kitchen, dressed up as a deadpan recommendation. That dry mockery is the textbook definition of sarcasm.

distractors.why_tempting: If you read 'life in microcosm' as a poetic flourish, A's 'Sentimental' can sound right — the line has a literary feel.
distractors.why_wrong: 'Life in microcosm' is offered alongside 'duty, boredom, bossiness and chaos' — hardly affectionate. Sentimental requires warmth or nostalgia; the writer offers neither. Step 3 keeps the mock-endorsement in view.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'characterised by duty, boredom' and assume the writer is reporting plainly — a prosaic style.
distractors.why_wrong: Plain reporting does not contain phrases like 'knock cookery off the pedestal' or 'as it should be' after a list of complaints. Those are rhetorical flourishes; prosaic tone is exactly what they aren't.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on a strong, opinionated piece can be to call it 'Moving' — emotionally charged.
distractors.why_wrong: Moving requires the reader to be invited into feeling. This writer is keeping the reader at arm's length with dry distance, and the closing endorsement of tedium is the opposite of emotionally stirring. Step 5 keeps the registers separate.

host-2017-verb1-ELF-038.technique: On tone items, find a sentence that ENDORSES something the writer obviously cannot mean ('as it should be' after listing negatives). That mock-endorsement is the strongest single signal of sarcasm. The trigger: a deadpan approving phrase attached to an undesirable list.
host-2017-verb1-ELF-038.pitfall: Sarcasm reads as plain statement to a fast reader — it only flips once you notice the mismatch between content and approval. The cure: ask 'would a normal cookery writer say this with a straight face?' If the answer is no, the writer is being sarcastic, not literal.

host-2017-verb1-ELF-039.solution_path: The Calorie Restriction paragraph says outright: 'a new 25-year-long primate study concluded that calorie restriction does not extend average life span in rhesus monkeys' and 'when it comes to calorie restriction in primates and people… the jury is still out.' So in humans, eating less does not appear to affect life expectancy. Answer A.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt asks what is true 'according to the text, with regard to calorie restriction in PEOPLE.' The scope is narrow: human evidence only. Watch out for options that quote the animal results without adjusting for the human qualifier.

steps.title: Locate the key sentences
steps.text: Three sentences carry the human-relevant claim. 'Reducing typical calorie consumption… extends life span by a third or more in many animals, including nematodes, fruit flies and rodents.' Then the pivot: 'When it comes to calorie restriction in primates and people, however, the jury is still out.' And: 'a new 25-year-long primate study concluded that calorie restriction does not extend average life span in rhesus monkeys.'

steps.title: Paraphrase in plain English
steps.text: In small animals, eating less extends life. In monkeys (the closest available proxy for humans), the new long study found no life-span extension. For humans specifically, the evidence is unresolved — but the most recent finding points to NO effect on average life span.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'The jury is still out' = the verdict is undecided, no conclusion yet. 'Average life span' = how long people live on average — not the same as health or quality of life. 'Life expectancy' (in option A) is the medical equivalent of 'average life span'. 'Calorie restriction' = eating substantially less than usual (here 30–40% less).

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: Walk each option. A ('Eating less does not appear to affect life expectancy') matches both the 'jury is still out' line and the new study's 'does not extend average life span' result — for primates, the closest human proxy. B says eating less LEADS to a longer life — true in nematodes and rodents, but the passage explicitly says it isn't established for people. C says the new study confirms PREVIOUS conclusions using a different method — actually it CONTRADICTS earlier monkey studies that suggested life extension. D says monkey results can't be applied to humans — the passage uses monkeys as evidence ABOUT humans, never disclaims the species transfer.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is A. For humans, the closest evidence (a 25-year primate study) shows no life-span extension, and the human jury is still out. The passage closes by noting health-span benefits — but on life expectancy specifically, A is the cautious, accurate read.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to carry forward the animal headline — 'extends life span by a third or more' — and apply it to people, especially because the paragraph starts with that finding.
distractors.why_wrong: The pivot word 'however' resets the claim for primates and people. Step 3's paraphrase isolates the human row: the evidence does NOT support B. Animal evidence does not transfer here.

distractors.why_tempting: If you remember 'a new 25-year-long primate study concluded…' as the latest piece of supporting evidence, C can read as 'new study confirms what we knew'.
distractors.why_wrong: 'A new… study concluded that calorie restriction does NOT extend average life span' contrasts with the previous 'some studies have suggested that monkeys that eat less live longer'. The new study contradicts, it does not confirm. Step 2 makes the contrast explicit.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'rhesus monkeys' and conclude the passage is warning against extrapolating from monkeys to humans.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage uses monkey data precisely BECAUSE it's the best stand-in for humans; nowhere does it caution against the transfer. The 'jury is still out' line refers to the data being inconclusive, not to a species mismatch.

host-2017-verb1-ELF-039.technique: On 'according to the text' items with a narrow scope ('in people'), strip every animal-only line out of consideration and answer only from the human-relevant sentences. The trigger: a pivot word like 'however' or 'when it comes to' that splits one population from another.
host-2017-verb1-ELF-039.pitfall: Calorie-restriction articles usually celebrate longevity findings, so the brain wants to read this one the same way. The cure: read the 'however' clause as a hard wall — once you cross it, animal data no longer counts.

host-2017-verb1-ELF-040.solution_path: The 'Democracy and Education' paragraph says education must produce 'an active, critical, reflective, and empathetic member of a community of equals' and warns we 'think far too little about what we need to do in order to transmit them to the next generation'. The fix is to take steps so young people grasp democratic ideas — exactly C.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt asks for the MAIN POINT argued in this text. So you need the single thesis the whole paragraph is built to support, not a side claim. Title-based items like this usually have an action recommendation hidden in the closing lines.

steps.title: Locate the key sentences
steps.text: Two passages frame the argument. Historical setup: education was 'remodelled to produce… an active, critical, reflective, and empathetic member of a community of equals'. Present-day complaint: 'We give these values lip service, but we think far too little about what we need to do in order to transmit them to the next generation and ensure their survival.'

steps.title: Paraphrase in plain English
steps.text: When democracy first emerged, schools were redesigned to make engaged democratic citizens. Today we still say we like democracy, but we don't actually do enough through education to pass those democratic habits to the next generation — so we need to take real steps to fix that.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Lip service' = praising something with words while doing nothing about it in practice. 'Transmit to the next generation' = pass on (here, through teaching). 'Self-evident' (option B) = obvious without needing to be argued. 'Devalue' (option D) = lower the quality or worth of something.

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: Walk each option. A says benefits of education should be spread more equally — the passage isn't about distribution; it's about the CONTENT of education (transmitting democratic values). B says democratic ideals are no longer seen as self-evident globally — the passage talks about lip service in 'we' (the writer's culture), not a global view-change. C ('In education, steps should be taken to make young people realize the importance of democratic ideas') maps onto the closing complaint that we 'think far too little about what we need to do… to ensure their survival'. D says democracy has devalued education — backwards; the passage worries about education failing to transmit democracy, not democracy failing education.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is C. The closing line is a call to action: we're not doing enough to transmit democratic values through schooling. The remedy that follows is exactly what C names — deliberate educational steps so young people understand democratic ideas.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read 'community of equals' as a call for equal access to education's benefits — a fairness reading.
distractors.why_wrong: 'Community of equals' here describes the goal of a democratic CITIZEN, not the distribution of education across society. Step 3's paraphrase keeps the focus on what students learn, not who gets to learn it.

distractors.why_tempting: If you remember 'we give these values lip service' as a sign that democracy is no longer taken seriously, B can sound like the modern diagnosis the writer is making.
distractors.why_wrong: The 'we' in the passage is the writer's own culture (where democracy is taken seriously rhetorically but not practically), not a global trend. B over-generalises to 'a global perspective' that the paragraph never adopts.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on the line 'we think far too little about what we need to do' can be to blame democracy itself for lowering educational standards.
distractors.why_wrong: The blame goes the OTHER way: education is failing to live up to its democratic mission. The passage never says democracy has devalued education — it says we've stopped teaching democratic habits well. Step 5 catches the inverted causal direction.

host-2017-verb1-ELF-040.technique: On 'main point' items in a short opinion paragraph, the thesis usually sits in the LAST sentence, often phrased as a complaint or recommendation. The trigger phrase: 'we give these values lip service' is a classic call-to-action setup — the verdict is that we should do more.
host-2017-verb1-ELF-040.pitfall: Short opinion pieces use historical setup to lead into a present-day complaint, and it's easy to mistake the setup for the main point. The cure: ignore the history paragraph for tone-only purposes and focus on the verb in the closing sentence — that verb (transmit, ensure, act) names the argument.

host-2017-verb2-ELF-031.solution_path: The sentence is 'Are you ever ___ to ask whether entities such as electrons… really exist?' The opening of a science essay is teasing the reader into curiosity, not commanding or convincing them. 'Tempted to ask' is the natural idiom for that nudge — answer D.

steps.title: Understand the gap type
steps.text: The gap is a verb in passive past-participle form: 'ever ___ to ask whether…'. Whatever fills it has to combine with 'to ask' to give a natural English phrase that captures how the reader might approach a question. The four candidates are all real English verbs in this slot, so the test is idiom + nuance, not grammar.

steps.title: Read around the gap
steps.text: Context: 'Are you ever ___ to ask whether entities such as electrons, black holes and the Higgs particle really exist? As a chemist I worry about what is real and dependable in my field.' This is the opening line of a popular-science essay — its job is to hook the reader by suggesting they might quietly wonder about something they normally take for granted.

steps.title: Identify the relationship
steps.text: The author is suggesting the reader feels a private, slightly mischievous pull toward a forbidden question (do these scientific things even exist?). That pull is internal and gentle — closer to 'curious about' than to 'told to' or 'argued into'. The right verb conveys an inner nudge, not external pressure.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Tempted to' = drawn toward, often to something you're not supposed to do — fits a forbidden question. 'Expected to' = required to, by social or professional norms — external, formal. 'Persuaded to' = talked into by someone else — requires an outside arguer. 'Supposed to' = under an obligation — duty-based, formal. Only 'tempted' captures a private inner pull.

steps.title: Plug and test each option
steps.text: A 'expected to ask' — wrong register; nobody is required by their job to ask whether electrons exist. B 'persuaded to ask' — needs an outside persuader who isn't in the sentence. C 'supposed to ask' — duty-based, but the question is taboo, not a duty. D 'tempted to ask' — perfectly fits the mischievous, private curiosity an essay opener wants to evoke.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is D. 'Tempted' carries exactly the right note: the reader is being invited to admit a quiet, almost forbidden curiosity. The whole essay then hangs on that hook.

distractors.why_tempting: If you read 'as a chemist I worry' as setting up a professional duty, 'expected to ask' can sound like a scientist's normal obligation.
distractors.why_wrong: Scientists are not expected to question the existence of well-established entities like electrons — that question is precisely the unusual one the essay wants to raise. Step 4's register check separates duty ('expected') from curiosity ('tempted').

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'Are you ever ___' and assume someone needs to talk the reader into the question, since the question is unusual.
distractors.why_wrong: 'Persuaded' needs an external persuader, and the sentence offers none. The opening is private — the reader, alone, feeling a pull. Step 3 keeps the pull internal.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on a science-essay opening can be to think the reader is 'supposed to' ask big philosophical questions — an obligation of intellectual life.
distractors.why_wrong: 'Supposed to' moves the question from temptation to duty, which kills the mischievous opener tone the writer is building. The whole essay frames realism vs. anti-realism as something the reader might daringly entertain, not as homework.

host-2017-verb2-ELF-031.technique: On cloze items at the start of an essay, ask: is the writer trying to RECRUIT the reader's curiosity (gentle, internal) or to COMMAND it (formal, external)? An opening hook usually wants internal pull words like 'tempted', 'curious', 'inclined'. The trigger: a rhetorical question opener almost always pairs with a soft inner-verb.
host-2017-verb2-ELF-031.pitfall: All four options fit grammatically, so the brain wants to call it a coin toss. The cure: read each option as a register check — does this verb name external pressure (expected, persuaded, supposed) or an internal pull (tempted)? The essay's voice picks the internal one.

host-2017-verb2-ELF-032.solution_path: The sentence is 'Our present theories are too ___ to have happened by chance: somehow we have latched onto the blueprint of the universe.' The claim is that the theories work too well to be a fluke. 'Too successful to have happened by chance' is the only option that supports that argument — answer C.

steps.title: Understand the gap type
steps.text: The gap is an adjective inside a 'too X to have happened by chance' construction. The adjective must name a quality of theories that, if extreme enough, makes a coincidence impossible — that is, a quality of WORKING, not just a quality of being interesting.

steps.title: Read around the gap
steps.text: Context: 'Scientific realism holds that if science has made great progress by invoking entities such as electrons, then we should take the next step of accepting that they really do exist… Our present theories are too ___ to have happened by chance: somehow we have latched onto the blueprint of the universe.' The previous sentence already names 'great progress' — the gap is a tighter restatement of that idea.

steps.title: Identify the relationship
steps.text: The argument structure is: science makes progress → progress is too big to be luck → therefore the theories are mapping something real. The missing adjective must restate 'great progress' or 'great accuracy' — something that names how WELL the theories work. Words about how 'interesting' or 'fancy' they are don't carry that weight.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Successful' = working well, achieving its goals — names performance. 'Speculative' = based on guessing rather than evidence — the opposite of solid. 'Abstract' = removed from concrete reality, intellectual — doesn't speak to performance. 'Simplistic' = too simple, missing real complexity — usually pejorative, also doesn't speak to working well.

steps.title: Plug and test each option
steps.text: A 'too speculative to have happened by chance' — speculation HAPPENING by chance is fine; the sentence wants something whose accuracy is too good for luck. B 'too abstract to have happened by chance' — abstractness is unrelated to chance. C 'too successful to have happened by chance' — fits the realism argument exactly: theories work so well they cannot be coincidence. D 'too simplistic to have happened by chance' — being simple says nothing about being correct.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is C. The whole sentence is the realists' headline: theories WORK too well to be a fluke. Only 'successful' connects working-well to the impossibility of chance.

distractors.why_tempting: If you remember the passage's later mention of theories and counter-arguments, 'speculative' can sound like the right neutral academic word.
distractors.why_wrong: Realists are defending science's TRACK RECORD, not its speculative flair. Step 4's register check is decisive: 'speculative' weakens the realist case, but the sentence wants something that strengthens it.

distractors.why_tempting: Scientific theories often are abstract, so 'too abstract to have happened by chance' can read as a familiar collocation.
distractors.why_wrong: Abstractness has no logical link to chance. The sentence specifically asks why something couldn't be coincidence — that's a question about performance, not about abstractness. Step 3 holds the argument structure in view.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on the line 'latched onto the blueprint of the universe' can be to picture a simple, elegant pattern — 'too simplistic' as a positive virtue.
distractors.why_wrong: 'Simplistic' carries the negative shade of 'too simple to be accurate', the opposite of what realists want to claim. And being simple doesn't argue against chance. Step 5's plug-and-test catches the meaning mismatch.

host-2017-verb2-ELF-032.technique: On 'too X to ___' cloze items, work out what quality of X would actually rule out the consequence. Here the consequence is 'have happened by chance', so X has to be a working-well quality — 'successful', 'accurate', 'effective'. The trigger phrase: 'too… to have happened by chance' always tests whether you can connect a quality to the impossibility of coincidence.
host-2017-verb2-ELF-032.pitfall: Several of the four options describe properties theories often have (abstract, speculative, simplistic), so the brain wants to pick whichever feels most 'science-y'. The cure: the adjective must specifically refute the chance hypothesis — only quality-of-working adjectives do that.

host-2017-verb2-ELF-033.solution_path: The sentence is 'Anti-realists accept the progress made by science but stop short of making the additional leap of ___ of believing in the materiality of things they cannot actually see.' The gap names what anti-realists refuse to grant — a religious-style commitment to unseen things. 'Faith' is the standard English word for that commitment — answer A.

steps.title: Understand the gap type
steps.text: The gap is a noun inside the structure 'making the additional leap of ___ of believing in…'. Whatever fills it must name what someone takes on when they 'believe in the materiality of things they cannot actually see'. That is exactly the textbook definition of one English word.

steps.title: Read around the gap
steps.text: Context: 'Scientific realism holds that… we should take the next step of accepting that they really do exist… Anti-realists accept the progress made by science but stop short of making the additional leap of ___ of believing in the materiality of things they cannot actually see.' The 'leap' framing echoes the religious idiom 'leap of faith' — and the very next phrase describes belief in unseen things.

steps.title: Identify the relationship
steps.text: Anti-realists refuse to make an extra commitment that, in religion, is called faith — believing in something you can't directly observe. The sentence is using a religious analogy on purpose: 'leap of faith' is a fixed English phrase, and the writer is leaning on it to characterise the realist's extra step.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Faith' = belief without (or beyond) direct evidence — exactly what believing in unseen entities means. 'Realism' = belief in things really existing — the position itself, not the leap toward it; would create circular phrasing. 'Proof' = evidence; you don't 'leap of proof' — proof is what you'd have if no leap were needed. 'Relevance' = bearing on a matter — semantically off entirely.

steps.title: Plug and test each option
steps.text: A 'leap of faith of believing in the materiality of things they cannot actually see' — the writer is invoking the idiom 'leap of faith' and immediately explaining it. Fits perfectly. B 'leap of realism' — the leap IS what produces realism; saying realists leap into realism is circular and the second clause goes nowhere. C 'leap of proof' — proof would eliminate the need to leap; the construction breaks. D 'leap of relevance' — meaningless in context.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is A. The sentence works because 'leap of faith' is a familiar phrase, and the writer is using it to dramatise what realists do extra: they grant a religious-style trust to scientific entities they can't see directly.

distractors.why_tempting: If you remember the surrounding debate as 'realism vs anti-realism', 'leap of realism' can feel like a clever way to name realism's commitment.
distractors.why_wrong: Realism is the position you arrive at after the leap, not the leap itself. The construction 'leap of realism of believing in…' becomes self-referential and never delivers a new idea. Step 5 catches the circularity.

distractors.why_tempting: Science is associated with proof, so 'leap of proof' can read as 'leap supported by proof' to a fast reader.
distractors.why_wrong: Proof is the opposite of a leap: if you have proof, there's no gap to leap across. The sentence specifically describes what people do when they DON'T have direct evidence of unseen entities. Step 3 holds the leap-vs-proof contrast in view.

distractors.why_tempting: If you're scanning quickly, 'relevance' can sound like a generic intellectual word that might slot anywhere.
distractors.why_wrong: Relevance has nothing to do with believing in unseen things, and 'leap of relevance' is not an English idiom. Step 4 isolates 'faith' as the only word whose dictionary meaning matches 'believing in things you can't see'.

host-2017-verb2-ELF-033.technique: On cloze items with the word 'leap' nearby, scan for the partner word the writer is leaning on as an idiom — 'leap of faith' is the canonical English phrase. The trigger: the idiom 'leap of X' is so well-worn that filling X almost always determines the answer.
host-2017-verb2-ELF-033.pitfall: All four candidates are abstract nouns from the same intellectual neighbourhood, so the brain wants to weigh them on feel. The cure: each option must produce a coherent metaphor with 'leap'. Only 'faith' produces an existing English idiom; the others produce phrases that don't work.

host-2017-verb2-ELF-034.solution_path: The sentence is 'Anti-realists also argue that their approach places them in a better position to ___ change when an established entity or theory becomes redundant.' The next sentence explains: 'allows them to move on to alternatives more easily'. Moving on smoothly when something becomes outdated is exactly 'adapt to change' — answer B.

steps.title: Understand the gap type
steps.text: The gap is a verb phrase followed by 'change'. The anti-realists are claiming an advantage of their stance, so the verb has to name a positive way of dealing with change — not resisting it, not creating it. The next sentence ('move on to alternatives more easily') gives the test answer almost directly.

steps.title: Read around the gap
steps.text: Context: 'Anti-realists also argue that their approach places them in a better position to ___ change when an established entity or theory becomes redundant. Not investing belief in a particular theory, they claim, allows them to move on to alternatives more easily.' The 'allows them to move on to alternatives' line is a glossary entry for whatever fills the gap.

steps.title: Identify the relationship
steps.text: When an old theory becomes redundant, anti-realists say they can switch fluidly to a new one. The verb must name graceful accommodation: receiving change and adjusting to it, not pushing it back or stepping it up. The phrase 'move on to alternatives more easily' = 'adapt to change'.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Adapt to' = adjust yourself to fit new conditions — the textbook positive response to change. 'Fend off' = ward off, defend against — describes RESISTING change. 'Step up' = accelerate, intensify — implies pushing harder, not adjusting. 'Account for' = explain, take into account — usually used for variables in an analysis, not for new theories you switch to.

steps.title: Plug and test each option
steps.text: A 'fend off change' — anti-realists are claiming AGILITY in the face of change, not resistance; the next sentence ('move on more easily') is the opposite of fending off. B 'adapt to change' — matches 'move on to alternatives more easily' word for word in meaning. C 'step up change' — anti-realists don't claim to drive change themselves; they claim to ride it out. D 'account for change' — would mean explaining why change happens, but the passage talks about responding to it, not explaining it.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is B. The follow-up sentence is the writer's own gloss: anti-realists 'move on to alternatives more easily' — which is precisely 'adapt to change'.

distractors.why_tempting: If you read 'better position to' as 'better armoured against', 'fend off change' can sound like a confident defensive stance.
distractors.why_wrong: Anti-realists in this passage are bragging about FLEXIBILITY, not armour. Fending off is exactly the realist stance ('stick with your theory'), not the anti-realist one. Step 3 keeps the direction of the argument in view.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on 'better position to' is to imagine an active push — 'step up change' as taking initiative.
distractors.why_wrong: The sentence describes what happens WHEN a theory becomes redundant — a passive trigger, not active initiative. Anti-realists are responding to change, not driving it. Step 5 catches the active/passive mismatch.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'when an established entity… becomes redundant' and imagine the anti-realist has to explain why — 'account for change'.
distractors.why_wrong: The follow-up sentence is about MOVING ON to alternatives, not about explaining redundancy. 'Account for' belongs in a discussion of why theories die, not in a discussion of switching to new ones.

host-2017-verb2-ELF-034.technique: When a cloze sentence is immediately followed by a 'because/since/which means' clause, that clause usually paraphrases the missing word. Read both sentences as one unit before choosing — the second sentence often hands you the answer. The trigger: 'allows them to…' is a near-perfect synonym signal.
host-2017-verb2-ELF-034.pitfall: All four phrasal verbs sound aggressive or active to varying degrees, which can pull the reader toward 'fend off' or 'step up'. The cure: the follow-up sentence is the writer's own gloss, and it says 'move on to alternatives more easily' — only 'adapt to' matches that motion.

host-2017-verb2-ELF-035.solution_path: The sentence is 'Science progresses by creeping up on the truth about the world: ___ successive theories were merely to replace one another, that progress would be truly miraculous.' The structure 'were merely to replace… that progress would be miraculous' is an inverted conditional, which needs 'if' as the connector. Answer D.

steps.title: Understand the gap type
steps.text: The gap is a subordinating conjunction at the start of a clause whose verb is in the subjunctive: 'were merely to replace one another'. That verb form ('were to + infinitive') is one of the marked signals of an English conditional. The connector has to be one that introduces such a conditional.

steps.title: Read around the gap
steps.text: Context: 'Realists retort such an approach is cavalier, or even dangerous. Science progresses by creeping up on the truth about the world: ___ successive theories were merely to replace one another, that progress would be truly miraculous.' The two clauses pair a hypothetical situation with its hypothetical consequence — pure conditional structure.

steps.title: Identify the relationship
steps.text: The realist is arguing: imagine, hypothetically, that successive theories were just replacing one another (with no real accumulation of truth). Then the apparent progress of science would have to be a miracle. The grammar pattern is 'if X were to happen, Y would happen' — the classic counterfactual conditional.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'If' = introduces a conditional, including the 'if X were to…' subjunctive form. 'Unless' = if NOT — flips the meaning. 'As' = in the same way that, or because — introduces comparison or cause, not condition. 'Although' = concessive — introduces a contrast, not a condition. Only 'if' fits the 'were to / would' template.

steps.title: Plug and test each option
steps.text: A 'unless successive theories were merely to replace one another, that progress would be truly miraculous' — flips the meaning: now miraculous progress would happen IN THE ABSENCE of replacement, which contradicts the realist's argument. B 'as successive theories were merely to replace one another' — 'as' here is wrong grammar with 'were to'; it doesn't introduce a counterfactual. C 'although successive theories were merely to replace one another, that progress would be miraculous' — concessive, doesn't pair with 'would'. D 'if successive theories were merely to replace one another, that progress would be truly miraculous' — perfect 'if + were to / would' structure, and the meaning fits the realist's reductio.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is D. The grammar — 'were merely to replace… would be truly miraculous' — is a textbook English counterfactual that requires 'if'. The argument the realist is making (the alternative theory would be absurd) lines up exactly.

distractors.why_tempting: If you remember the realist's stance as 'reject the anti-realist view', 'unless' can sound like the natural sceptical hedge.
distractors.why_wrong: 'Unless' reverses the conditional, turning the realist's reductio into its opposite (miraculous progress would happen if theories did NOT just replace each other). The argument now contradicts itself. Step 5's plug-and-test makes the flipped meaning visible.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct can be to read 'as' as a causal connector — 'because/since' — pairing the two clauses naturally.
distractors.why_wrong: 'As' doesn't fit with the subjunctive 'were to replace' / 'would be' pair; that pattern needs an 'if'. With 'as' the sentence loses its counterfactual force and the second clause's 'would' becomes ungrammatical.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'Realists retort…' and assume the next sentence contains a concession — 'although' as the contrasting setup.
distractors.why_wrong: 'Although' introduces a real contrast, not a counterfactual. It doesn't combine with 'were to / would'. The realist is constructing a hypothetical, not granting a concession.

host-2017-verb2-ELF-035.technique: On cloze items where the verb form is 'were to' followed by 'would', the connector is almost always 'if'. That triple (if + were to + would) is the marked structure of an English counterfactual conditional. The trigger pattern: spot 'would' in the main clause and 'were to' in the subordinate clause — they pair across an 'if'.
host-2017-verb2-ELF-035.pitfall: Conjunction cloze items invite reading by sense rather than by grammar, so 'unless' can sneak in if you're following the sceptical mood. The cure: identify the verb forms first ('were to', 'would') and pick the connector that grammatically licenses both — only 'if' does.

host-2017-verb2-ELF-036.solution_path: The passage says that in the interwar years 'women were employed as typists and in lower-grade clerical roles in embassies, but never as the professional equals of men.' Working in support roles without professional status equals B — background tasks, no public duties.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt asks for the most ACCURATE description of women's role in the interwar period. So the right option must cover what women DID do (some role existed) and what they did NOT do (something was off-limits). Options that are too broad ('did everything') or too narrow ('did nothing') will lose.

steps.title: Locate the key sentence
steps.text: The decisive sentence: 'By the interwar years, women were employed as typists and in lower-grade clerical roles in embassies, but never as the professional equals of men.' Two clauses, two facts: they DID work — clerical and typing roles, in embassies. They did NOT work as professional equals — no senior or representative roles.

steps.title: Paraphrase in plain English
steps.text: Between WW1 and WW2, women could work in British embassies but only behind the scenes — typing letters, filing, supporting the men. They were never put forward as diplomats themselves, never sent to negotiate or represent Britain. Support roles, yes; public diplomatic duties, no.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Clerical' = office work, paperwork, low-grade administrative tasks. 'Professional equals' = colleagues of the same rank — here, full diplomats. 'Public duties' (option B) = visible representative work like negotiating, attending official functions, signing treaties. 'Background tasks' = behind-the-scenes work.

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: Walk each option. A says women worked in offices in Britain but rarely abroad — the passage explicitly places them 'in embassies', i.e., abroad; A is geographically wrong. B ('background tasks but not permitted to perform any public duties') matches the typist-and-clerical line plus 'never as professional equals'. C says women were not allowed to be involved in any type of work — too strong; they did clerical work. D says they played active roles in diplomatic negotiations unofficially — the passage gives no examples of unofficial diplomacy; women's role was clerical, not shadow-diplomatic.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is B. The interwar picture in the passage is precise: women had jobs in embassies, but those jobs were typing and clerical — never the public, representative work of a diplomat. B captures both halves of that fact.

distractors.why_tempting: If you remember 'Foreign Office' as 'an office in Britain', A can feel right — women in London offices.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage explicitly places women 'in embassies' — that is, in British missions abroad. Step 3's paraphrase keeps the geography: support work happened both at home and overseas, just not at diplomatic rank.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at the strong wording about exclusion ('never as the professional equals of men') and conclude no women were involved at all.
distractors.why_wrong: The same sentence first says women WERE employed, just as typists and clerks. 'Not equals' is not the same as 'not present'. Step 5 keeps both halves of the sentence in view.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on a piece about hidden women's history is to assume they did diplomatic work informally that wasn't credited.
distractors.why_wrong: This passage doesn't supply that story. The text says women did clerical work — it doesn't claim they conducted secret negotiations. D imports a hidden-contribution narrative the passage doesn't contain.

host-2017-verb2-ELF-036.technique: On 'most accurate description' items, the right answer usually has TWO halves — naming what was permitted AND what was forbidden. Single-clause options (everything / nothing) tend to be wrong because the historical reality is almost always partial. The trigger phrase 'but never as…' is gold: it signals exactly the partial picture.
host-2017-verb2-ELF-036.pitfall: It's easy to swing to one extreme — either 'they did real diplomatic work' or 'they did nothing at all'. The cure: hold both halves of the sentence (employed AS clerks BUT not as equals) and pick the option that uses both.

host-2017-verb2-ELF-037.solution_path: Vansittart said admitting women 'would not be in the interests of the women themselves' and would damage British prestige; Smith swiftly pledged to gather 'all the ammunition I can get' against the same idea. And of 51 ambassadors, only three favoured women. The two men's views were similar AND representative of most ambassadors — answer B.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt asks how the views of Vansittart and Smith are BEST DESCRIBED. So the right option must capture both men's positions accurately — relative to each other AND relative to other officials. Watch for options that exaggerate a difference between them.

steps.title: Locate the two men's positions
steps.text: Vansittart: appointing female diplomats 'would not be in the interests of the women themselves, nor in the interest of His Majesty's Service; and it would be damaging to the prestige of His Majesty's Government in foreign countries'. Smith: handed the task by Vansittart, 'swiftly pledged to gather "all the ammunition I can get"'. And the broader context: 'Of 51 ambassadors canvassed for their opinions, only three were in favour of employing women.'

steps.title: Paraphrase in plain English
steps.text: Vansittart wanted women kept out and dressed up his opposition in language about women's own interests and national prestige. Smith got the assignment from Vansittart and threw himself enthusiastically into building the case against women — gathering 'ammunition'. Two men, same goal. And almost every ambassador (48 of 51) agreed with them.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Pledged' = committed; gave a strong promise. 'Ammunition' here is metaphorical — arguments and evidence to use against the proposal. 'Reflected those of most ambassadors' (option B) = lined up with what most ambassadors thought. 'Radical' (option C) = far from the centre, extreme. The two men were CENTRE of the conservative consensus, not on its edge.

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: Walk each option. A says Vansittart MORE THAN Smith considered women's interests — the passage never compares them on this axis; Vansittart's 'women's own interests' line is paternalistic boilerplate, not actual consideration. B ('most likely similar and reflected those of most ambassadors') matches Vansittart's opposition + Smith's eager assistance + 48-of-51 ambassador agreement. C says Smith was MUCH MORE RADICAL than Vansittart — the passage gives no evidence of a divergence between them; they worked together against the proposal. D says their views differed FROM those of British ambassadors — the opposite of 48-of-51 alignment.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is B. Vansittart and Smith are presented as a team executing the same conservative position, and the wider ambassador poll shows that position was the institution's overwhelming consensus — not an outlier view.

distractors.why_tempting: If you read 'would not be in the interests of the women themselves' as genuine concern for women, Vansittart can look more sympathetic than Smith.
distractors.why_wrong: The 'women's own interests' line is a rhetorical shield, not a real consideration — Vansittart deploys it inside a list of reasons to exclude women. Step 3 keeps the substance in view: both men were working against women's appointment. The passage offers no contrast between them on sympathy.

distractors.why_tempting: Smith's vivid 'all the ammunition I can get' makes him sound more aggressive than Vansittart's measured statement.
distractors.why_wrong: Smith's enthusiasm describes his METHOD (eager assembly of evidence), not a more extreme POSITION. They share the goal; only the language is more colourful. Step 5 separates style from stance.

distractors.why_tempting: If you focus on the Foreign Office as central and ambassadors as peripheral, D can read as 'central officials vs. distant ambassadors'.
distractors.why_wrong: The poll number is decisive in the other direction: 48 of 51 ambassadors AGREED with Vansittart and Smith. Their views WERE the ambassador consensus. Step 5's count is the cleanest disproof of D.

host-2017-verb2-ELF-037.technique: On items comparing two named people, scan first for explicit contrasts between them (passage signals: 'unlike', 'whereas', 'in contrast'). If no contrast exists, the right option is usually one that emphasises similarity. The trigger: a statistic ('only three were in favour') usually places the two men inside or outside that consensus — use the statistic.
host-2017-verb2-ELF-037.pitfall: When one named figure speaks in measured prose and the other in vivid metaphor, the contrast in STYLE can be mistaken for a contrast in SUBSTANCE. The cure: ask whether the passage credits them with different GOALS; if not, the difference is cosmetic.

host-2017-verb2-ELF-038.solution_path: Vansittart said: 'It is a false argument to say that, because women are treated as equals in this country, an Englishwoman abroad will be so treated by foreigners.' By labelling the equality claim 'false argument' he was conceding that equality WAS the assumed picture at home — Britain had domestic equality, but it wouldn't carry abroad. Answer A.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt asks what Vansittart CLAIMED about the status of men and women in 1930s Britain. The answer must be a claim he made about the DOMESTIC British situation, not about foreign ones. Watch out for options that mix up his domestic picture with his foreign-posting concerns.

steps.title: Locate Vansittart's claim
steps.text: The key sentence: 'It is a false argument to say that, because women are treated as equals in this country, an Englishwoman abroad will be so treated by foreigners.' The premise he is rejecting is the leap from domestic to foreign equality — but the domestic claim ('women are treated as equals in this country') is taken as given, not disputed.

steps.title: Paraphrase in plain English
steps.text: Vansittart's logic is: 'Yes, in Britain women are treated as equals — fine. But that doesn't mean a British woman sent abroad would be treated as equal by foreigners.' He is granting the domestic equality and only contesting the inference from it. So his domestic claim is: no status gap at home.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'False argument' here doesn't mean 'the premise is false' — it means 'the inference from premise to conclusion doesn't go through'. The premise (women as equals in Britain) is granted; the inference (therefore foreigners will treat them equally) is rejected. This kind of logical distinction is often the load-bearing wall of comprehension questions.

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: Walk each option. A ('There was no difference in status between British women and British men') matches the granted premise 'women are treated as equals in this country'.
